tier: Semantic vs. Instance vs. Panoptic Segmentation
title: Choosing Your Segmentation
concept: Choosing the right segmentation method depends on whether you need class-level maps, individual object boundaries, or a complete pixel-level environment map.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the distinction between 'stuff' and 'things' in panoptic segmentation?

options.text: 'Stuff' refers to uncountable background zones; 'Things' are countable objects
'Stuff' is processed by GPUs; 'Things' are processed by CPUs

exercises.explain: Correct! 'Stuff' is background (sky, road), and 'things' are countable instances (cars, people).

cards.title: Matching task to tool
cards.body: Imagine you are hired by an agricultural tech company. You need to write a model for a drone. Should it identify weeds vs. crops (semantic)? Count individual oranges on trees (instance)? Or map the entire orchard including soil and sky (panoptic)? Choosing wrong wastes time and money.

cards.title: Choosing the right type
cards.body: Select Semantic when you need general area masks without counting. Select Instance when you need to detect and outline individual items. Select Panoptic for a complete, dense layout of the entire scene.

cards.title: Cooking, plating, and the whole restaurant
cards.body: Semantic is like cooking a soup: all ingredients merge. Instance is like placing three distinct steaks on plates. Panoptic is managing the entire table setting: the soup, the steaks, the table, and the plates together.

cards.title: Medical MRI scans
cards.body: In medical imaging, if you want to highlight the liver, semantic segmentation is perfect (one class). If you need to segment three distinct kidney stones to measure their sizes individually, you use instance segmentation.

cards.title: More complex isn't always better
cards.body: You might think you should always use panoptic segmentation because it does everything. However, panoptic models are complex, require massive datasets, and are slow. If you only need to find lane markers, simple semantic segmentation is far better.

left.text:
Best for: Land cover, lanes, organs
No instance counts
Fastest and simplest

right.text:
Instance: object contours & counts
Panoptic: complete scene mapping
Higher compute cost

exercises.prompt: If you need to label the sky, clouds, and grass in landscape photos without counting them, which model should you use?
exercises.explain: Correct! Since you don't need to count clouds or grass patches, semantic segmentation is the best choice.

exercises.prompt: True or False: You should always choose panoptic segmentation because it is the most complex model.
exercises.explain: False. Simpler models like semantic segmentation are faster and easier to train if you don't need instance tracking.

exercises.sentence: To count and outline individual cells under a microscope, you should use ___ segmentation.
exercises.explain: Instance segmentation is required to distinguish and count individual cell boundaries.

exercises.prompt: Which of these matches a panoptic segmentation use case?

options.text: A full-scene virtual reality system that maps the floor, walls, and every individual furniture piece
An app that checks if a photo contains a cat
A system that draws boxes around speed limit signs

exercises.explain: This maps background zones (floor, walls) and individual objects (furniture), representing panoptic segmentation.

exercises.prompt: You are designing the software for a vacuum cleaning robot. It needs to know which pixels are carpet vs. tile (to adjust suction), and it must outline individual obstacles like toys and shoes so it doesn't run over them. Which is best?

options.text: Panoptic segmentation, because it maps background textures ('stuff') and identifies individual obstacles ('things') at the same time.
Semantic segmentation, because we don't care about the shoes.
Instance segmentation, because the robot doesn't need to know about the floor.
Object detection, because bounding boxes are enough to change suction.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The robot needs carpet/tile zones (semantic) and individual toy boundaries (instance). Combining them into panoptic segmentation is the perfect solution. Phase 9: Image Segmentation is complete. Next up: Advanced Vision Applications.
